sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, I can see the player has moved LEFT and is now overlapping with the dark gray T-shape! The player is near the divider, and the dark gray T is also near the divider. The yellow L-shape is now further to the right, not overlapping with the player.
This is interesting. The player and dark gray T are now adjacent/overlapping. Let me check the yellow L pixel count and see if the dark gray T is being consumed or if something else is happening.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Nodes:")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"  color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Check yellow L pixel count
yellow_l = 0
timer_yellow = 0
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'Y':
        if any(p[1] == 63 for p in node['boundary']):
            timer_yellow = node['pixels']
        else:
            yellow_l += node['pixels']
print(f"\nYellow L: {yellow_l} pixels")
print(f"Timer yellow: {timer_yellow} pixels")

# Check player position
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'B' and node['pixels'] > 30 and node['pixels'] < 50:
        print(f"Player: boundary={node['boundary']}")
        break

# Check dark gray T position
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['color'] == 'c':
        print(f"Dark gray T: boundary={node['boundary']}")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 24-29 (3x6=18 pixels)
- Vertical stem: rows 48-53, cols 24-26 (6x3=18 pixels)
Total: 18 + 18 = 36 pixels
But the initial dark gray T had 45 pixels. Let me check the initial boundary: [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44], [17, 39], [18, 38], [23, 38], [23, 36]].
- (15, 36) to (15, 44): top edge of horizontal bar (cols 36-44, 9 columns)
- (15, 44) to (17, 44): right edge of horizontal bar (rows 15-17, 3 rows)
- (17, 44) to (17, 39): bottom edge of horizontal bar (cols 39-44, 6 columns)
- (17, 39) to (18, 38): transition from bar to stem
- (18, 38) to (23, 38): left edge of stem (rows 18-23, 6 rows)
- (23, 38) to (23, 36): bottom edge of stem (cols 36-38, 3 columns)
- (23, 36) to (15, 36): left edge of horizontal bar and stem (rows 15-23)
So the initial dark gray T had:
- Horizontal bar: rows 15-17, cols 36-44 (3x9=27 pixels)
- Vertical stem: rows 18-23, cols 36-38 (6x3=18 pixels)
Total: 27 + 18 = 45 pixels
But the current dark gray T has:
- Horizontal bar: rows 45-47, cols 24-29 (3x6=18 pixels)
- Vertical stem: rows 48-53, cols 24-26 (6x3=18 pixels)
Total: 18 + 18 = 36 pixels
The horizontal bar shrank from 9 columns to 6 columns (3 pixels lost on the right side). The vertical stem is still 3 columns wide. So 9 pixels were consumed from the horizontal bar.
This makes sense because the player is adjacent to the dark gray T at cols 29-30, rows 45-47. The dark gray T's horizontal bar ends at col 29, and the player's horizontal bar starts at col 30. The interface is at cols 29-30, rows 45-47.
The dark gray T's horizontal bar is 3 rows x 6 columns = 18 pixels. The initial horizontal bar was 3 rows x 9 columns = 27 pixels. So 9 pixels were consumed from the right side of the horizontal bar.
This suggests that the player consumes adjacent objects at the interface. The more the player overlaps with the object, the more pixels are consumed.
OK so now I understand the mechanic better. The player consumes adjacent objects. The goal is to consume all pixels of the dark gray T and yellow L-shape.
But the consumption is temporary - when the player moves away, the objects reassemble. So the goal might be to consume all pixels simultaneously, or to achieve a specific configuration.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the player can only consume one object at a time (since the dark gray T and yellow L-shape are far apart). So the goal might be to consume one object completely, then move to consume the other object.
But the consumption is temporary, so this doesn't make sense. Unless the consumption becomes permanent after a certain threshold or duration.
Let me try a different approach. Let me move the player so it's adjacent to the yellow L-shape and see if the dark gray T reassembles while the yellow L-shape is consumed.
